session_summary: Developer asked for design of optimal candidate-set logic and morning cleanup UX to minimize tasks sent to task classification. Claude performed three targeted web searches on daily planning tools, task management prioritization, and RAG retrieval techniques (recall vs precision). Research surfaced the two-stage retrieval pattern from production RAG systems: Stage 1 builds a wide morning "day working set" (~10–20 tickets from 33 open) once daily using existing signals (in-progress status, recently-touched tickets, due dates, graph neighborhoods from pm_tasks and ticket_links); Stage 2 compresses to ~5–8 per-episode candidates via semantic reranking against episode summary using pm_task_embeddings, with committed-today and recently-linked tickets pinned to the floor. Morning UX modeled on Sunsama's planning ritual: system proposes the working set, developer triages keep/drop/add in ~30 seconds, marks 1–3 as committed (WIP-limited for predictability), with triage written idempotently to pm_task_curation. Two recall safety-valves prevent morning-net incompleteness from starving the classifier: high-similarity bypass pulls in unplanned-but-obvious matches above threshold from the full 33-ticket universe, and untracked items get clustered and proposed as candidates the next day, creating self-healing feedback loop. Design directly addresses "fewest tasks sent to classifier" goal (~5–8 vs 33) while protecting recall upstream via wide initial net and graph-based discovery, not by starving the classifier. Existing schema fields (pm_task_embeddings, pm_tasks, ticket_links) already support the design; implementation is wiring only. Claude noted two prerequisites: pm_task_embeddings must be populated and episode summaries must be clean, and offered to next specify Stage-1 signal weights and exact morning UI shape.

Continuing from earlier discussion of task classification and episode grouping, user shifted focus to the candidate-set problem: minimizing tasks sent to the classifier while maintaining recall safety. Agent performed three web searches on daily-planning UX patterns, Jira task surfacing, and RAG reranking techniques. From these results, agent synthesized a two-stage retrieval model: Stage 1 builds a "day working set" of 10–20 tickets from 33 open ones (using signals like in-progress status, recent activity, due dates, graph neighborhood from existing pm_tasks/ticket_links tables, and yesterday's untracked items). Stage 2 compresses this to a 5–8 ticket episode candidate set for the classifier using semantic similarity reranking against pm_task_embeddings, with pinned/recent tickets and "untracked" always included. Agent included two safety valves: high-similarity bypass to surface unplanned-but-obvious matches against the full ticket universe, and untracked→tomorrow feedback loop so the working set self-heals. Agent emphasized this reduces classifier context noise (per RAG literature) while keeping recall safe upstream rather than starving it.

User then requested creation of two Jira tasks: (1) "Clean pm tasks candidates for classification" (research rules, then implement; due today), and (2) "Task classification audit and accuracy improvement" (due tomorrow), both assigned to "adithya." User asked agent to understand existing epics, determine fit, break down tasks/subtasks as needed, and follow project-management conventions.

Agent began execution: read stored Jira account reference (meridiona.atlassian.net, KAN project, account ID prefix noted) and attempted to load Atlassian MCP tool schemas but hit empty ToolSearch result.

Developer asked whether all companies maintain sprints, recognizing that the previous candidate-set design leaned on sprint signals. Claude ran two targeted web searches on sprint adoption across software teams. Research revealed ~65% of teams use sprints (meaning ~35% don't), with Scrum adoption declining sharply from 87% (2022) to the 20s%, as teams shift toward Kanban and continuous-flow methods. Notably, 85–90% of engineers prefer Kanban over Scrum. The developer's own board is a Kanban board with empty sprint fields, making the sprint-dependent design a blocker. Claude immediately adapted the candidate-set logic in response: demoted sprint from a required tier signal to an optional booster, and identified three universal signals that work on all boards (status_raw='In Progress', recent episode task_keys, and morning declaration). Updated Tier-1 to derive from these universal signals with sprint as a bonus precision layer when present. Noted that morning declaration becomes more critical on no-sprint Kanban boards, which otherwise lack a strong "what's active today" signal. Consolidated findings into a revised design table showing how signals map across sprint vs. Kanban teams. Offered next step: write a single consolidated design document before coding, or continue probing edge cases.

Agent executed the task-creation request by first surveying the KAN board's epic landscape via JQL to identify the right parent for both tasks. It queried all epics, retrieved children of KAN-174 "Accuracy & Automated Deep-Eval" and others, and searched for existing tasks related to candidate selection, classifier accuracy, and audits. The agent determined that KAN-174, owned by Aditya and already containing the classifier-accuracy work (KAN-109, KAN-199 baseline, KAN-200, KAN-202), was the correct live home—no new epic was needed. It then fetched issue-type metadata to map custom fields: Start date → customfield_10015, Due date → duedate.

The agent created two parent Tasks under KAN-174. KAN-230 "Clean candidate-set selection feeding the task classifier" (due 2026-06-15, high priority, assigned to Adithya Harish) was decomposed into three subtasks: KAN-232 (research & document selection rules), KAN-233 (implement two-stage pipeline: recall-wide day working set plus per-episode reranking), and KAN-234 (morning cleanup UX for keep/drop/add). KAN-231 "Audit task-classification accuracy and improve it" (due 2026-06-16, high priority, assigned to Adithya Harish) was broken into KAN-235 (measure accuracy and build failure taxonomy), KAN-236 (root-cause top failure clusters), and KAN-237 (implement fixes and re-measure against baseline). All six subtasks were assigned matching due dates to their parents. The agent then linked both parent tasks together (KAN-230 ↔ KAN-231, "Relates") and linked KAN-231 to the baseline task (KAN-231 ↔ KAN-199, "Relates") for traceability and comparative measurement. Task descriptions embedded the constraint that candidate selection is input curation (allowed), while classification decisions remain fully LLM-driven.

Developer asked Claude to design a morning task-setup flow for the Meridian PM board tool, aiming for automated PM updates without friction. The goal: devs declare morning tasks → system captures work + suggests untracked items belong to existing tasks or need new ones. Claude ran three web searches on developer plan adherence, automatic time-tracking tools (Timely, Memory.ai), and Jira time-logging friction. Research validated the approach and revealed a critical UX principle: never ask devs to author, only to confirm pre-filled suggestions. Evidence showed manual Jira worklogs miss 15–40% of real time, devs deviate from plans constantly due to interruptions, and tools that draft suggestions (not blank forms) achieve 75% reduction in tracking friction. Claude reframed the flow: morning = one-tap confirmation of pre-filled list (carry-over from yesterday's active tasks + In Progress status), evening = batched review of untracked items with suggestions (attach to existing task, create new task, or dismiss). Never blocking on morning step was a core requirement. Developer approved the approach and asked for the next step: UI design for morning setup across both new-user onboarding and existing-user daily flows. Claude attempted a Bash query to verify codebase surfaces (tray, dashboard, onboarding structure) but hit a temporary classifier outage; user resubmitted the request. Claude worked around the blocker using schema knowledge from CLAUDE.md and delivered a complete morning-setup design with a "Today" widget component that renders identically for both user types but with different pre-fill sources: existing users carry over yesterday's task_keys + In Progress items (1 tap to confirm), new users see a smart-sorted top-8 from the board by In Progress status and recent activity (3–4 taps to pick their daily set). The design framed picking as the implicit cleanup signal so new users never face a separate "exclude" chore. Edge cases absorbed: afternoon opener (one gentle prompt then auto-run), Monday/time-off (offer to carry over previous), no-tracker wedge users (free-text labels), finished tasks (offer status transition). Claude then posed two decisions to finalize before implementation: primary surface choice (tray popover for daily 1-tap, dashboard for richer onboarding), and whether to use implicit cleanup (picking = keeping) or explicit exclusion step on onboarding.

Claude investigated the existing ticket-triage cleanup engine for the morning task-setup flow, confirmed it re-runs correctly on every PM sync (so reopened tickets in Jira + re-sync handles staleness automatically), and ruled out the proposed "decision re-surfacing on material change" as unnecessary since the PM tool owns the lifecycle. User clarified that assignee filtering is already in place and surfacing drift is deferred. Claude then designed and fully implemented a morning "Today's tasks" feature: a new migration (`041_daily_plan.sql`) with `daily_plan` and `daily_plan_meta` tables, a scoring library (`ui/lib/daily-plan.ts`) that pre-fills suggestions from carryover/in-progress/recent work weighted by due date, an API route (`ui/app/api/plan/route.ts`) supporting GET (plan + suggestions + scored board) and POST actions (confirm/add/remove/reorder/skip/reopen), and a two-column drag-and-drop UI (`PlanView.tsx` + supporting components) using `@dnd-kit`. The initial implementation compiled and passed basic API tests but had critical drag-and-drop bugs: right-to-left always appended to bottom instead of respecting drop position, and left-to-right drag (removal) wasn't handled. Claude spawned an external reviewer agent which audited the component and identified the root cause (mixed `useDraggable`/`useSortable` with prefixed ids causing drop-target loss and missing board droppable zone) plus eight additional issues (no optimistic rollback, DragOverlay sizing, state-sync desync, accessibility gaps). Claude then completely rewrote `PlanView.tsx` using the canonical dnd-kit pattern (unified ids, `closestCenter`, board container as drop target for removal), added optimistic rollback on failed saves, unified the live-edit API to a single `set` action, fixed accessibility (aria-labels, proper form semantics, drag announcements), removed confusing UI states, and added drop-zone visual feedback. The rebuilt version compiled clean, all POST actions verified working end-to-end (confirm → positions, add → position, reorder, remove, reopen, skip), and the page renders without errors. Feature is live on the dev server (`localhost:3939/plan`, keyboard shortcut `8`).

This session focused on improving the plan page's drag-and-drop implementation and enriching task cards with PM-relevant context. Claude replaced the problematic hand-wired dnd-kit implementation with @hello-pangea/dnd (the maintained fork of react-beautiful-dnd, 30k★) after researching and reading official documentation to confirm correct API usage. Dependencies were swapped (`@dnd-kit/*` uninstalled, `@hello-pangea/dnd@18.0.1` installed), and PlanView.tsx was rewritten using the canonical `DragDropContext`/`Droppable`/`Draggable` pattern with documented `onDragEnd(result)` handler shape (`source`/`destination {droppableId, index}`). Code compiled clean on first build after the swap.

Claude then enriched card context by adding description excerpts, epic names, status, priority, story points, and issue type from the existing `pm_tasks` schema. A new `/api/plan/task?key=` endpoint was created to serve full descriptions (2293+ chars) and acceptance criteria for a click-triggered task detail dialog, matching the existing `HygieneDialog` modal pattern with backdrop blur, Escape-close, and a tracker link. The card render logic was deduplicated: origin chips now only show for signals other chips don't convey (carryover / worked-recently), suppressing redundancy when status or due pills already communicate the same fact.

The page was re-themed to white & blue (scoped to avoid repainting the app-wide orange brand), with primary buttons colored blue and "Due today" / "Overdue" pills switched from amber to red. Dialog background scroll was locked via `body.style.overflow = 'hidden'` cleanup on close. The board's independent scroll was restored (`max-h-[64vh] overflow-y-auto`), and the Today column was made sticky (`lg:sticky lg:top-6`) so it remains reachable when dragging from a scrolled board.

A Next.js 16.2.6 code review confirmed no framework anti-patterns: `force-dynamic` is valid (Cache Components isn't enabled), `new URL(req.url).searchParams` is correct for route handlers, and all helper functions are actively used with no dead code. The page had a critical liveness bug: it fetched once on mount and never refreshed, risking stale board data after PM syncs. Claude added a 30-second background poll (`load(false)`) that refreshes board suggestions without re-deriving the Today list, protecting user edits from clobbering. The poll is skipped during active drag (tracked via `draggingRef`), and save-error rollback now re-seeds from server truth. This follows the dashboard's polling convention and ensures the board stays live while user work is never corrupted.

User asked how Claude Code can test, see, and iterate on UI it writes, requesting research on available plugins. Claude conducted web searches for "Claude Code test UI visually Playwright MCP Chrome DevTools MCP screenshot 2026" and "Claude Code see browser take screenshot iterate frontend plugin MCP", retrieving results on browser-driving tools and testing frameworks. Claude analyzed the landscape, identifying three main options: Playwright MCP (Microsoft's default, ~33 tools for navigation, clicking, screenshots, a11y snapshots, console/network logging), Claude in Chrome (Anthropic's extension with ~16 tools for real-time visual feedback in live browser sessions), and Chrome DevTools MCP for performance profiling. Claude noted token cost implications — browser tasks via MCP cost ~4× more than CLI-based approaches due to full a11y tree and screenshot streaming. Claude reviewed existing Meridian repo capabilities including `run`, `verify`, `meridian-ui`, `cua-driver`, and `frontend-design` skills, noting these already handle UI testing without new plugins. Claude recommended adding Playwright MCP for screenshot and DOM verification of the Next.js dashboard, leveraging existing `verify`/`meridian-ui` skills for the launch-and-check loop, with Claude-in-Chrome as a backup for quick visual tweaks. User then invoked `/chrome` command with no output, leaving the conversation incomplete.

User reported an unwanted outer page scrollbar on the `/plan` route. After analyzing the layout, I identified it was a height-context problem: the `DashboardShell` component was built for document-flow pages with a tall footer, making all pages scroll; the `PlanView` had two columns with asymmetric height constraints (the board was capped at `64vh`, the Today column was uncapped), causing both an outer and inner scrollbar. Fixed with flexbox height distribution—the correct architectural approach rather than magic numbers. Modified `DashboardShell.tsx` to add a route-scoped app-like context for `/plan` that binds the viewport to `h-[100svh] overflow-hidden` with no footer (all other routes unchanged); rewrote `PlanView.tsx` to use `flex-1 min-h-0` for the grid and both droppables, replacing the `max-h-[64vh]` cap and dropping `min-h-[140px]` constraints. Marked headers, titles, and action rows as `shrink-0` so they stay fixed-size while the scroll areas claim remaining height. Build succeeded. Separately, user encountered a "failed to run migrations" error in the Tasks sync UI. Diagnosed as a **stale binary issue unrelated to the plan changes**: the installed CLI binary at `~/.meridian/app/bin/meridian` is from Jun 11 and doesn't embed migrations 36–40 (confirmed via `strings`), while the database has been migrated forward to v40 by a newer daemon. The sqlx migrator refuses to run when the DB contains migrations the binary doesn't know about. Migration 041 (added earlier in this session) isn't even involved—that binary predates it. Offered to rebuild the binary from current source and improve error messaging in `main.rs:265` to show the full error chain rather than the generic message.

Following successful completion of a 30-second background polling implementation for the plan page that ensures board state stays fresh without clobbering user edits or active drags, the developer compacted the conversation context to manage token usage. The polling refactor added a guarded `load(false)` mechanism that skips refresh during drag operations (tracked via `draggingRef`), applied SSE/polling per codebase convention (polling for data views, SSE for streams only), and concluded with a clean build (`✓ Compiled successfully in 1291ms`). Context compaction preserved the full transcript while freeing capacity for pending work: wiring the committed daily_plan into the classifier as Tier-1 priority (to boost committed tasks in candidate selection), which remains explicitly deferred pending user confirmation.

Continued multi-week daily plan UI build by addressing data-refresh logic. User questioned whether SSE was appropriate for the plan page's task board. Analysis revealed the prior implementation fetched data only on mount and never refreshed, causing new PM sync tickets to not appear until page reload. Corrected the approach by implementing a 30-second background poll in `ui/components/views/PlanView.tsx` aligned with existing dashboard conventions (TodayView already polls on 30s interval; SSE is reserved for true streams like health/notices/logs). The poll implementation uses a `draggingRef` guard to skip refreshes during active user drag operations, protecting in-progress edits from being clobbered by board updates. The load function was refactored with useCallback to distinguish initial load (seeds Today list and handles errors) from poll refreshes (updates board suggestions only, leaving Today edits independent). useEffect sets up the poll on mount with cleanup on unmount. Save error rollback now calls full load to re-seed from server truth. Encountered a type error when onClick handler passed MouseEvent to `load(initial?: boolean)`; resolved by wrapping with lambda `onClick={() => load(true)}`. Build succeeded cleanly with `npm run build` producing no type or compiler errors in 1291ms.

The session delivered three major features across a full-stack refactor. First, the developer reported a UX gap: the Plan page's Confirm button entered "live mode" allowing silent auto-saves on drag, contradicting the expectation that "Confirmed" meant locked. The agent investigated the confirm flow and redesigned it using a lock + Edit/Done model: after Confirm, dragging/removing/adding are disabled; an "Edit plan" button unlocks for explicit "Save changes" / "Cancel" (no silent saves). State management (`confirmedMode`, `editing`) was reworked, drag/remove handlers gated on `editable`, and TaskDialog updated to hide add/remove when locked. Build clean, PR #288 opened.

Second, the agent wired the daily_plan table into the classifier's candidate ranking (the intended payoff of the Plan page). Added `_local_day()` to convert session UTC timestamps to local days, `_fetch_plan_focus()` to retrieve confirmed task keys, and reworked `_fetch_pm_tasks()` to float focus tasks first and tag `is_today_focus`. Prompt updated with ★ markers: "prefer ★ only if the evidence fits" (boost, never filter). All candidates still flow (verified: 2 focus + 21 follow). PR #289 committed stacked on #288.

Third, the developer requested a production-grade notification system. The agent researched existing code (daemon notices + hardcoded tray health/pause), designed a transactional outbox architecture, and shipped PR #290 across four phases: (1) migration 042 + src/notifications.rs emit API; (2) tray relay draining `/api/notifications/pending`, delivering via tauri-plugin-notification, acking delivered rows, **plus the critical fix: requesting macOS notification permission on startup** (the root cause of silent drops); (3) UI routes + banner + Settings extensions (per-type toggles + quiet-hours); (4) three event sources wired (plan.nudge, worklog.ready, system.fault). Removed hardcoded worklog dedup. Fixed pre-existing clippy lint. All phases compiled clean, integration tests pass. End-to-end smoke test confirmed delivery works: test row inserted → both API endpoints returned it → tray relay drained and acked within 30s.

Continuing earlier work on three major features, the developer tested the notification system end-to-end and uncovered a macOS-specific rendering issue: toasts weren't appearing despite the outbox pipeline working correctly. Root-cause diagnosis revealed two separate suppressions: (1) the dev tray runs as a loose binary, but macOS only renders notifications from signed `.app` bundles, and (2) screenpipe's screen recording triggers macOS "sharing the display" mode which silently suppresses all notifications unless explicitly toggled in System Settings. After the user enabled the mirroring toggle, the developer built and launched the bundled Meridian Tray.app (via `npm run tauri build`) and confirmed native toasts render correctly. They then added permission walkthrough guidance to both installers (install.sh and install-from-bundle.sh), opening the Notifications settings pane and instructing users to enable "Allow notifications when mirroring or sharing the display," matching the existing pattern for Screen Recording/Accessibility/Microphone permissions. Next, the developer implemented a policy endpoint `GET /api/notifications/allowed?event=<key>` (reusing the same quiet-hours helpers) so that the tray's direct health and pause/resume toasts now respect the master switch, per-type toggles, and quiet hours uniformly with the outbox notifications—the tray queries the endpoint before firing, but fails open if the dashboard is unreachable so critical alerts still fire. The endpoint was validated returning `{"allowed":true}` under current prefs. Finally, the developer addressed all five findings from the code review on PR #288 (daily plan UI): (1) fixed input validation so malformed `date` on writes returns 400 instead of silently writing to today, (2) fixed error handling so GET failures throw and trigger `loadFailed` instead of being masked as empty days, (3) eliminated double board-scoring in POST by pre-scoring once and passing `available` to `buildPlanResponse`, (4) dropped the redundant `idx_daily_plan_date` index from migration 041 (PK's leftmost column already covers it), and (5) consolidated the duplicated `dueDaysFrom` helper by exporting it from lib/daily-plan and importing it in the task route. UI built clean, migration tests passed. The developer declined the optional suggestion to memoize plan focus (cache staleness risk outweighed minor query savings) and posted reasoning on PR #289. Both PRs received summary comments on the review threads. The session closed with four open PRs: #291 (dev binary fix, independent), #288 (plan UI, ready), #289 (classifier boost, stacked), #290 (notifications, verified).

Debugged and fixed a critical dev-tooling bug blocking the Tasks → Sync button. The issue was a binary version mismatch: `dev-start.sh` runs the daemon via `cargo watch -x 'run --bin meridian'` producing a fresh `target/debug/meridian` that had applied all migrations to meridian.db, but the UI's sync route handlers called `selectMeridianBinary()` which resolved to a stale installed binary at `~/.meridian/app/bin/meridian` (Jun 11) that lagged behind the DB, causing sqlx to abort with "failed to run migrations". Initially copied the current dev build over the stale installed one as a one-off patch. Then implemented a durable fix by making `meridianCandidates()` environment-aware: in development (NODE_ENV === 'development'), it now prepends `<repo>/target/debug/meridian` ahead of installed paths, ensuring the UI and daemon always shell out to the same build. Production behavior unchanged — installed-only, native-first, preserving launchd PATH parity. Modified three files: `ui/lib/meridian-bin.ts` (dev-aware resolution logic), `ui/__tests__/meridian-bin.test.ts` (added four dev-mode tests, pinned existing tests to production), and `src/main.rs` (improved error reporting with `{e:#}` to print the full sqlx source chain instead of opaque "failed to run migrations" message). All 12 unit tests passed; build succeeded in 1298ms. Created branch `fix/dev-meridian-binary-resolution` off `main`, committed with pre-commit/pre-push validation passing, and raised PR #291 as a focused dev-tooling fix independent of the two open feature PRs.

The developer fixed sidebar keyboard shortcut numbering on the daily-plan-today-tasks branch, where the Plan route was wired as key 8 but appeared second visually, scattering the sequence 1-2-3-4-5-7-8. The agent read Sidebar.tsx, reassigned all kbd values sequentially 1–8 in visual order (Today through Settings), updated the Settings button label from 6 to 8, then fixed DashboardShell.tsx's KEY_ROUTES constant and footer hint (now "1–8 to switch view"). The UI compiled clean and the commit pushed successfully to the branch.

The user then reported the MLX classifier server down with no UI warning. Claude diagnosed a hung uvicorn --reload process (pid 19491, ~3h uptime) — the /health endpoint timed out after ~8s and the request log hadn't grown in ~2 hours, leaving 776 sessions pending classification. The root cause was likely a model reload wedging during a branch switch or heavy build. The critical gap: the daemon had no active health monitoring. It only raised mlx.down reactively when a classify attempt failed, so a wedged/idle server stalled silently without surfacing on the UI, tray, or notifications.

To fix this, Claude switched to feat/notifications-system (where the notices→outbox bridge already existed) and added a proactive MLX health probe to the daemon's main poll loop. The probe uses the existing fast intelligence::mlx_ready() check (3s timeout, model-free) every cycle to raise/clear mlx.down. Claude unified the severity to "warning" to keep the banner color consistent. One mlx.down raise now surfaces on three channels: dashboard NoticeBar (always shown), system.fault outbox row (→ tray toast, pref-gated), auto-clearing on recovery. The daemon compiled clean (clippy passed) and the fix shipped in commit feece88 on #290.

Claude manually injected test rows to verify end-to-end surfacing: the "Classifier offline" banner appeared on the dashboard and the tray successfully delivered the toast within 30s. The fix transforms a silent stall into immediate visibility across all user-facing channels.

User asked whether code comments were left in the earlier dev-tooling fix. Claude confirmed that explanatory comments were added to three files in commit 740fd5e (now in PR #291): `ui/lib/meridian-bin.ts` (JSDoc on why dev prefers the repo build), `src/main.rs` (comment on the {e:#} error-chain printer), and `ui/__tests__/meridian-bin.test.ts` (path context note). Claude also clarified that no inline PR review comments were added, only the PR description. The user was on branch `feat/daily-plan-today-tasks` at that point.

The conversation then shifted to debugging why the Python MLX server wasn't responding. Claude ran process and network diagnostics, discovering that while the uvicorn --reload supervisor process (PID 19491, running 3 hours) held port 7823, every HTTP request timed out with HTTP 000 in ~7-12 seconds. Memory usage was only ~15 MB (ruling out a loaded MLX model). Further investigation revealed the supervisor had no actual application worker child process—only a multiprocessing.resource_tracker—meaning the worker had crashed. Claude diagnosed the root cause as a worker crash (likely triggered by a syntax or import error in `services/agents/`, which the `--reload` watcher caught), leaving the supervisor as a hollow shell accepting connections but unable to serve. Claude offered two paths forward: either the user could manually restart by pressing Ctrl-C in the "MLX server (uvicorn --reload)" Terminal window and rerunning, or Claude could kill the process and restart the server from the current shell. The session ended with that offer pending.

After resuming from context compaction, the developer requested creation of a Jira task to track the session-task classifier plan-boost logic. The agent created **KAN-239** ("Pass confirmed daily plan (today's tasks) to the session-task classifier as Tier-1 candidates") via the Atlassian `createJiraIssue` API, setting type=Task, parent=KAN-174 (Accuracy & Automated Deep-Eval epic), assignee=Adithya Harish, duedate=2026-06-16, and priority=Medium. The agent then invoked `editJiraIssue` to set customfield_10015 (Start date) to 2026-06-16. A verification query confirmed all fields landed correctly. The agent noted that the core implementation is already shipped on main (PRs #289 → #294, release 1.53.1), so the ticket documents completed work + the follow-up eval-Goldens validation. The agent asked whether to transition KAN-239 to Done or leave it in To Do, awaiting user direction.

Following up on earlier work, Claude reviewed PR #290 (notifications system) against a detailed review from Akarsh-Hegde. The review flagged six findings: error logging suppression, React cleanup leak, JSON parse instrumentation, ID validation, interval teardown, and native toast deep-link asymmetry. Claude checked out the `feat/notifications-system` branch and systematically addressed each flagged location in the source. Real bugs were fixed: (1) `src/pm_worklog/scheduler.rs:254` changed from `let _ = enqueue()` to `if let Err(e)` with logging, mirroring the plan-nudge path; (2) `ui/components/NotificationBanner.tsx` added a `retryRef` with `cancelled` guard to prevent the SSE reconnect timer firing after unmount. Correct input tightening was applied: (3–4) dismiss and delivered routes now reject `id <= 0`, and the banner-store interval teardown now stops the shared 30s poll when the last subscriber leaves. Judgment calls were documented rather than implemented: the native toast click-navigation gap (v1 limitation, flagged as acceptable) got a comment at `notify()`, and tray JSON logging was skipped because the tray has zero logging infrastructure (`tracing`/`log`/`eprintln!` absent). All changes were validated: daemon `cargo check` (5.42s), tray `cargo check` (3.72s), and UI `npm run build` (1939ms) all succeeded; pre-push suite passed. Commit `e34e80d` was created and pushed to `feat/notifications-system`, then Claude posted a point-by-point reply to the review on GitHub identifying which findings were fixed and why two were intentionally left. Claude then reviewed PR #292 (MLX model idle eviction) by reading the full diff and verifying concurrency safety by tracing the global Rust `llm_gate`, confirming all four production model paths go through `model_session()` with `_in_flight` protection, and checking that test fixtures reset module globals. The review was detailed and specific: eviction cannot free the model mid-inference because `_in_flight` is set before `_get_model()` runs, the `mlx_ready` semantics were unchanged (already keyed on `loaded_at`), and the evictor never stalls inference. Claude identified one pre-existing concurrency invariant (daemon's single gate protecting non-gated HTTP callers) and three minor nits (memory attribution terminology, low eviction thrash risk, unnecessary evictor spawn at `MLX_IDLE_EVICT_S=0`), and explicitly cleared three candidate failure modes (race, leak, cache leakage). The review was posted to PR #292 with all context and no blockers flagged.

The user reported that after installing Meridian via `curl | bash` bootstrap, the permission toggles for screenpipe and meridian a11y were not appearing in macOS System Settings, unable to grant Screen Recording or Accessibility permissions. The agent investigated installation scripts and permission handling, initially identifying this as expected behavior for unsigned CLI daemons, which don't auto-populate the toggles. When the user reported that manually adding the binary via the `+` button in System Settings didn't work, investigation deepened. The agent confirmed the screenpipe binary at `~/.meridian/bin/screenpipe` was a real Mach-O executable and had the user run `screenpipe record`, which reported permissions granted but returned a "port already in use" error due to an already-running daemon. Running `meridian doctor` revealed that screenpipe was running (pid 34267) but capturing zero frames because the vision manager enumerated zero displays and failed to start VisionManager. A clean `meridian restart` was attempted but the log still showed `0 enumerated` monitors and the same error. Critical diagnostics revealed a permission identity mismatch: when run from Terminal, `screenpipe vision list` successfully enumerated "Display 1", but the launchd daemon process saw zero monitors. This proved the Screen Recording grant was attached to Terminal.app's identity, not screenpipe's own daemon identity. The persistent failure of the `+` button was traced to a known macOS Sequoia bug that silently refuses to add bare CLI binaries (without app bundles). Multiple remediation attempts were made including `tccutil reset ScreenCapture`, `tccutil reset Accessibility`, and `launchctl bootstrap` commands, but none restored functionality. The root cause was identified as the screenpipe binary being unsigned; macOS TCC cannot grant Screen Recording to unsigned CLI binaries, preventing the daemon from establishing its own identity and registering with the system. The agent provided a comprehensive fix: check the binary signature with `codesign -dv`, ad-hoc sign it if unsigned with `codesign --force --sign -`, strip quarantine flags with `xattr`, reset the grants, and restart the daemon to allow it to self-register with TCC.

User reported that the plan page card for KAN-240 (a closed task) was rendering as a bare key with no title, description, status, or epic—while other tasks rendered fully. Claude-code investigated by reading the plan page component (`PlanView.tsx`), the API route, and the data-loading library (`daily-plan.ts`), discovering that `loadPlan` performs a `LEFT JOIN` to `pm_tasks` and falls back to showing just the task key when there's no match. Investigation of the database confirmed KAN-240 was absent from `pm_tasks` despite being in `daily_plan`. The root cause was traced to the Jira sync provider (`src/intelligence/providers/jira.rs`), which fetches tickets with JQL `statusCategory != Done` and then prunes any `pm_tasks` rows not in the fetched set. Once KAN-240 went Done, it dropped off the board and was pruned, even though the daily plan still referenced it. Claude-code designed a fix that "pins" daily-plan-referenced Jira tickets: the sync now collects task keys from `daily_plan` that belong to known Jira projects and appends `OR key IN (…)` to the JQL, so planned-then-completed tickets are re-fetched regardless of status, upserted with their real terminal state, and survive the prune. Implementation added `is_valid_jira_key()` to validate keys before JQL interpolation (blocking injection attacks and non-Jira plan keys), modified the `fetch()` function signature to accept `pinned_keys`, updated the call site in `refresh_if_stale()` to compute and pass those keys, and added a unit test for the validator. All 9 Jira tests and clippy passed. The fix requires daemon rebuild and restart to take effect on the next Jira sync.

User asked why the classifier-debug OpenObserve dashboard (ID 7472580234202578944) was showing empty. I recommended three fixes in priority order: (1) widen the window to 6h or 24h in the URL or OpenObserve time picker (6h is the dashboard's intended default), (2) account for ingestion lag (OO traces lag real time, so a 10m window can miss classifies from 3+ minutes ago), (3) verify the empty filter variables aren't pinning unintended constraints. Continuing from earlier dashboard diagnosis work, the user reported that OpenObserve traces for classified sessions were showing many session blocks in a single trace, contradicting an earlier PR that aimed for one-trace-per-session isolation. The user also expected traces to show which screenpipe frames contributed to each classification.

Claude investigated the code and identified the root cause in the daemon's classifier drain loop: `src/main.rs` was parenting every `run_task_linking` and `run_coding_agent_classification` call under one shared poll/startup tick span using `.instrument(parent_span.clone())`, which collapsed N sessions into a single trace with N sibling `classify_session` blocks. The earlier PR had only removed the batch-wrapper span but never made each session its own root trace. Frame attribution (which frames fed a classification) had never been implemented at all.

After confirming scope with the user, Claude created branch `feat/per-session-classify-trace` and implemented a three-part fix: (1) made each classification a root trace by removing `.instrument(parent_span)` and passing a `tick_link` parameter to both functions instead, allowing navigation back to the triggering tick via a span Link without nesting; (2) applied the same per-session root-trace pattern to `run_coding_agent_classification` which has an internal loop; (3) added `min_frame_id`, `max_frame_id`, and `frame_count` to the Python classifier's SQLite SELECT and stamped them on the `db_fetch` span to show which screenpipe frame window fed each session.

The changes touched four files: `src/main.rs` (removed `.instrument()`, computed `tick_link` from the parent span, updated two call sites, removed now-unused `use tracing::Instrument as _`), `src/intelligence/task_linker/mod.rs` (added `tick_link` parameter to both functions, added root-span logic in the coding-agent loop), `tests/task_linker_smoke.rs` (updated 6 test call sites to pass `None`), and `services/agents/run_task_linker_mlx.py` (extended the SELECT query with frame columns and set them as span attributes on `db_fetch`).

Verification passed: clippy clean with `-D warnings`, three `task_linker_smoke` tests passed (two ignored, require live MLX), Python syntax check passed, pre-commit hook passed. Commit 9b918cb was created and pushed to origin; PR #304 was opened.

For live validation, the dev daemon (running under cargo-watch) auto-rebuilt into the fix around 13:00. An OpenObserve query over the last 30 minutes confirmed all three parts working: every trace from 13:00:30 onward shows exactly one `classify_session` per trace (vs. older merged traces with 2, 3, 4+ sessions), each `run_task_linking` span carries a non-empty link with `link.kind=poll_tick` pointing to the triggering tick, and `db_fetch` spans now display frame ranges. The user's initial confusion—seeing "4" next to `classify_session`—was clarified: those are child-node counts (the four children: `db_fetch`, `classifier_input`, `llm_inference`, `classifier_output`), not session counts. The fix is production-ready and safe to merge.

Claude investigated why screenpipe failed to start when running `bash dev-start.sh` after `meridian stop`. Through inspection of `meridian-cli.sh` and `dev-start.sh`, Claude identified the root cause: `meridian stop` calls `launchctl disable` on the screenpipe launchd agent, which permanently clears its `KeepAlive` flag and prevents automatic restart. Meanwhile, `dev-start.sh` assumed screenpipe was always running via launchd in the background and had no code to re-enable it after disable, leaving screenpipe dead.

Claude fixed this by editing `dev-start.sh` to add a screenpipe re-enable sequence before launching the dev Terminal windows, using `launchctl enable`, `bootstrap`, and `kickstart -k` — the same pattern used by `meridian dev screenpipe`. However, during user testing, the developer reported that `dev-start.sh` was taking an unusually long time after the fix. Claude diagnosed this as unnecessary blocking caused by the `launchctl kickstart -k` call, which forcefully kills and restarts the process, then waits for full initialization — a slow operation for screenpipe due to macOS screen-recording permission grants. Since `meridian stop` already terminated screenpipe and the plist has `RunAtLoad = true`, the `enable + bootstrap` sequence alone is sufficient to start the process without the blocking delay. Claude removed the `kickstart -k` call and added an explanatory comment, eliminating the unnecessary startup delay while keeping screenpipe recovery functional.

Investigated why the MLX classifier server appeared offline despite the UI showing "Classifier offline" banner. Initially diagnosed a client-side timeout during a 546-second worklog generation (40,118 chars), but user confirmed the server was truly down. Checked live process state (PID 65456 supervisor, 65606 worker) and discovered the real culprit: the worker process was wedged on a bootstrap pipe read with only 8 MB RSS and 0.02 seconds CPU time accumulated over 86 minutes, meaning the 7 GB Qwen model never loaded. Sampled the worker's stack trace confirming it was blocked in `_buffered_readline` → `read()` on the multiprocessing bootstrap pipe, unable to proceed past interpreter startup. Diagnosed root cause: `uvicorn --reload` (dev-only flag running the actual app in a subprocess) had recycled the worker when memory pressure spiked during the long synthesis, and the respawn produced a dead-on-arrival worker wedged on inter-process communication. The supervisor kept the listening socket open, masking the fact that no working application was behind it. Meanwhile, the 3-second health check timeout in `src/health/mlx.rs:14` treats any timeout as "server down," creating a false-positive "Classifier offline" banner when the server is actually just busy. Identified two fixes needed: (1) drop `--reload` from production/dev server launch to prevent respawn-wedge recurrence, and (2) distinguish connection-refused (truly down) from timeout-while-listening (busy) in the health check so the UI doesn't cry offline during heavy load. Offered to restart the server immediately (kill 65456/65606, relaunch cleanly) and implement both fixes on a branch with no commit.

Debugged and fixed concurrent GPU contention between MLX classifier and Jira worklog synthesizer. The root cause was a process-local Rust semaphore (`llm_gate`) that resets on daemon restart, allowing the Python server to receive concurrent model requests after the daemon restarts while classify is still running on the GPU. Implemented three fixes: (1) added an `asyncio.Semaphore(1)` in the Python MLX server's lifespan, then wrapped all three model-calling endpoints (`/classify_sessions`, `/v1/chat/completions`, `/summarise`) to acquire the semaphore before threadpool calls, ensuring serialize-at-server rather than relying on a daemon-restartable gate; (2) raised `PM_WORKLOG_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_S` from 300 to 900 seconds so the HTTP client can wait up to 15 minutes for long-running classifies; (3) added exponential backoff (5s, 10s) between retry attempts in the worklog synthesizer to avoid hammering a recovering server. Verified syntax and confirmed no deadlock risk (worklog endpoint does not directly hold the semaphore, only acquires it indirectly through agno's `/v1/chat/completions` callback). Pre-push checks passed (cargo fmt, cargo clippy, cargo test, ui tests, security audit). Created feature branch `feat/mlx-server-serialize-worklog-classify`, committed with a cargo fmt fix for a Rust file (semicolon style), pushed to origin, and opened PR #313. All three fixes deployed together to close the GPU saturation gap without breaking existing code.

Instead implemented the serialization fix: added `asyncio.Semaphore(1)` to guard all three model-calling endpoints (`/classify_sessions`, `/v1/chat/completions`, `/summarise`) before their `run_in_threadpool` calls, ensuring classify and worklog synthesis never compete on the GPU even across daemon restarts.
